__data__.text:
In question 42, the recommen-
dation (“Unknown”) and the voting (69% agreement) indicate that this is
a largely unresolved question .
Question 46 asked if the in vitro sterilization method should be
the same as that used in vivo. The recommendation is “Yes” and 93% of
the voters agreed . Sterilization can affect the physical, chemical, and
mechanical properties of the intended implants or materials, which is
critical for softer materials such as polymers, resorbable materials,
antibacterial constructs, and metals.
3,4 The choice of sterilization
technique is preferably determined by its impact on clinical
performance and commercial viability. For example, if a sterilization
technique would negatively or unpredictably affect device antimi-
crobial performance, then it would be unacceptable clinically. From
the perspective of moving a new antimicrobial technology to-
ward commercialization, it would make sense to determine steriliza-
tion viability as early as possible, as it pertains to device performance.
Question 49 examined whether existing International Safe
Transit Association (ISTA)/American Society for Testing and Materi-
als (ASTM) standards can be used for constructing biofilm models on
medical devices. The recommendation is “No,” with 80% agreement .
The ASTM International has implemented standardized methods,
guidelines, and specifications for the accurate and reproducible
formation of biofilms and testing of antimicrobial substances, which
are however mainly used for environmental biofilms. Four types of
biofilm devices have been addressed: the drip flow reactor and
rotating disk reactor to evaluate biofilm formation in a continuous
flow under low and medium shear stress; the Calgary Biofilm Device
(CBD; ASTM E2799 ‐17) and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC)
biofilm reactor (ASTM E2562 ‐17, ASTM E3161‐18, ASTM E2871‐19)
for evaluation of disinfectants; and the colony biofilm model to grow
and quantify Bacillus subtilis biofilms (ASTM E3180 ‐18).
5 An ASTM
symposium on “Antimicrobial combination devices ” in 2020 held a
session on methods for quantifying biofilms and methods for
assessing antimicrobial efficacy in biofilm eradication. 6,7 Despite
these efforts, there remains an unmet need to standardize methods
and techniques for the evaluation of clinically relevant biofilms. The
existing ASTM standard test methods may serve as a model to derive
such methods.
3.3 | Methodology: Biofilm characterization
Question 39 and Question 48 examined whether there were
methods to determine minimum biofilm eradication concentrations
(MBEC) and whether there was a standard method to remove
bacteria from surfaces.
Question 39 asked if there is a best in vitro method for assessing
MBEC. It was noted that there are many conditions that are used to
form biofilms, usually developed for nonmusculoskeletal indications
514 | HICKOK ET AL .
 1554527x, 2024, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jor.25774, Wiley Online Library on [03/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

metadata.file_name: Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Hickok - The 2023 Orthopedic Research Society s international consensus meeting on.pdf
metadata.file_path: E:\Guideline-driven-AI\backend\..\guidelines\Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Hickok - The 2023 Orthopedic Research Society s international consensus meeting on.pdf

__data__.text:
and that MBEC determination is highly dependent on the specific
method, surface, and medium. Crucial methodological variables to
consider and report
8 include the choice of bacterial species and
strains (clinical isolates vs. laboratory ‐acclimated), inoculum prepara-
tion and quantitation, and conditions for biofilm formation, antibiotic
challenge, and recovery (time, media, pH, temperature, fluid dynam-
ics, etc.). These variables also control biofilm maturity which can
affect MBEC results. Thus, relative biofilm maturity can be assessed
functionally by measuring MBECs for an antibiotic whose activity is
known to vary with biofilm maturity. Additional variables are
introduced in methods for quantitation of bacterial survival. Finally,
the purpose of standardized methods is to be able to predict clinical
efficacy. To the best of our knowledge, no correlations exist between
MBEC values for antimicrobial agents assessed by different methods
and their efficacy in treatment of orthopedic device infections, nor
have in vivo studies to address this question been performed. This
would be a very valuable area of future studies to help decide the
best standard method(s) for MBEC testing. Thus, a recommendation of
“No” was given, with 82% agreement with the recommendation .
Question 48 addressed if there is a standard method to detach
and quantify bacteria attached to surfaces. The question was
explored in two parts. 1. For test coupons, sonication was the most
common method for recovering bacteria while other enzymatic or
chemical methods were also available. 2. For recovery from a tissue
around an explant, homogenization was the most common. Based on
this research, it was concluded that there was not a standard method for
detaching and quantifying surface bacteria (82% agreement) .
3.4 | Conceptual: Understanding concepts
in bacterial tolerance and pathogen evolution
Questions 36, 37 , and 45 were questions regarding the under-
standing of bacterial tolerance and pathogen evolution and testing
concepts to match this understanding. While it is widely acknowl-
edged that “biofilms” are associated with higher tolerance for drugs
and are thought to result in greater treatment challenges, in vivo data
on the characterization of bacterial tolerance are scarce.
9 Changes in
antibiotic susceptibility have been attributed to the presence and
changes in the biofilm matrix, changes in bacterial metabolism,
increases in efflux pump activity, and alterations of antibiotic target
and bacterial membrane permeability to antibiotics.
Question 36 asked if antibiotic tolerance is an indicator for the
presence or maturity of a biofilm. There was strong consensus (79%
agreement) with the “No” recommendation. Laboratory studies support
that biofilm maturity increases generally with age for cultures up to 3
weeks.
10 The degree of tolerance is dependent on the antimicrobial
agent, the species, the treatment exposure time and the model system.
Thus, since the degree of tolerance depends not only on the age of the
biofilm but many other factors,
11 it was recommended that a single
measurement is not sufficient to determine whether a biofilm is mature.
Question 37 asked if drug clearance and protein binding could be
modeled in vitro to predict drug efficacy. Caution was recommended
when predicting in vivo antimicrobial susceptibility using in vitro
platforms, especially those that lack the factors that mimic the in vivo
environment in the host. Specifically, to predict the concentration of
given drugs in the plasma and local tissues of interest, one or two ‐
compartment pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) models
with variations for route of administration and/or clearance mecha-
nism are commonly used and are necessary as part of the
development of new antibiotic drugs.
12,13 There were no PK/PD
models for antibiotics using intraarticular administration. Further-
more, there is very little information on biofilm characteristics in vivo.
However, the authors concluded that such modeling, given the
appropriate information, could be used to predict in vivo efficacy, and
recommended that the question be answered “yes.” (75% agreement) .
Question 45 asked if small colony variants (SCV) or persisters
should be detected in clinical samples. Although both persisters and
SCV refer to bacteria populations that persist in the presence of
antibiotic stress, persisters revert to wild‐type populations upon culture
in the absence of antibiotics.

metadata.file_path: E:\Guideline-driven-AI\backend\..\guidelines\Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Hickok - The 2023 Orthopedic Research Society s international consensus meeting on.pdf

__data__.text:
12,13 There were no PK/PD
models for antibiotics using intraarticular administration. Further-
more, there is very little information on biofilm characteristics in vivo.
However, the authors concluded that such modeling, given the
appropriate information, could be used to predict in vivo efficacy, and
recommended that the question be answered “yes.” (75% agreement) .
Question 45 asked if small colony variants (SCV) or persisters
should be detected in clinical samples. Although both persisters and
SCV refer to bacteria populations that persist in the presence of
antibiotic stress, persisters revert to wild‐type populations upon culture
in the absence of antibiotics. Auxotrophy is associated with SCV while
tox/anti‐tox mechanisms are generally associated with the persister
phenotype. SCV, which are detectable by morphology and colony size,
have been reported to constitute 2% – 20% of clinical populations
14
while persisters are difficult to detect in clinical isolates. 15,16 Several
methods involving colony size and differential staining are in develop-
ment for the detection of persisters. 16,17 While there is currently no
correlation between the presence of SCV and difference in treatment
outcomes,
18 persisters in general are associated with chronic infections
which have shown lower responses to antibiotic therapy. The
recommendation, based on current evidence was “Unknown” and 72%
agreed. There is a clear gap in the tools to detect persisters in clinical
scenarios due to their reversion to wild ‐type characteristics in culture,
and the general lack of standard methods/definitions for detecting
SCVs and persisters. Finally, because the antibiotic concentrations to
which the bacterial populations are exposed are crucial in determining
bacterial fate, the modeling of accurate antibiotic concentrations in
vitro with simulated in vivo factors is important and needs further
research.
3.5 | Conceptual: Host/bacteria/biomaterial
interactions
Questions 40 and 47 investigated bacterial ‐host interactions.
Question 40 asked if the concept of the “race for the surface ”
was still valid. The race for the surface posited that a combination of
proteins, proteoglycans and host cells would cover a biomaterial
surface, protecting it against colonization by bacteria. The time,
number, and type of cells present; the types of biomacromolecules in
the local environment; and the surface properties of the implant play
significant roles in determining, in the short term, the dominance of
bacteria or host cells that colonize the implant.
19,20 The authors
suggested that a race may not exist based on the literature that they
reviewed. Host cells appear to colonize first and depending on the
strain of bacteria, either protect or succumb to a bacterial challenge.
HICKOK ET AL . | 515
 1554527x, 2024, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jor.25774, Wiley Online Library on [03/02/2026]. metadata.file_name: Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Hickok - The 2023 Orthopedic Research Society s international consensus meeting on.pdf
metadata.file_path: E:\Guideline-driven-AI\backend\..\guidelines\Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Hickok - The 2023 Orthopedic Research Society s international consensus meeting on.pdf

__data__.text:
As such, the conclusion was that there was NOT a race for the surface,
with 58% agreement .
Question 47 addressed whether there are any rigorous in vitro
bone cell models that can be generalized for the study of intracellular
bone infections. A systematic review approach was used to analyze in
vitro models of various cell types relevant to bone infection. Cell
types discussed included mouse primary osteoblasts, calvarial
osteoblasts, and osteoclasts; osteocyte cell lines, human mesenchy-
mal progenitor cells, osteocytes, and primary osteoblasts, human
osteoblast and osteocyte ‐like cell lines, human chondrocytes, and
synovial fibroblasts. It was concluded that S. aureus , at least, could
exist intracellularly. The models, using monolayer cultures of cells,
varied in fidelity, with greatest confidence in primary cell models.
Furthermore, it was recommended that controls should include
noninternalizing bacterial strains, a method (and demonstration
thereof) for clearance of extracellular bacteria, and characterization
of the time and multiplicity of infection dependence of the model.
With these various controls, it was concluded that there were rigorous
models for studying bacterial internalization in osteoblastic lineage cells
(85% agreement). It was noted that studies with other musculoskeletal
cell types were not sufficiently rigorous.
4 | DISCUSSION
In vitro assessments are critical for understanding MSKI and for
screening possible therapies. While many studies seek to define
antimicrobial properties, these studies are limited, and the in vitro
section responses highlight this resultant high degree of variability.
The questions that achieved the highest consensus (Q. 43,
outcome measures [97%] and Q. 46, sterilization method [93%])
indicate that target system ‐dependence must be considered. These
recommendations integrate with the “No” recommendation for Q. 49
(79%) that concludes that existing ISTA/ASTM methods are
inadequate for medical biofilm models. Together, these emphasize
further need for flexibility and better definition of critical
conditions.
21
Recommendations of 1.5 – 3 log reduction for designating anti-
bacterial efficacy rely on relatively few studies and highlight the need
for integrated in vitro/in vivo experimental design. Other questions
indicate that biofilm controls are necessary, but defining bacterial
inocula, antibiotic tolerance, biofilm maturation, MBEC conditions,
strain choice and breadth, and even detachment methods can be
highly variable with gaps in knowledge of the relevance of in vitro
methods to in vivo outcomes.
Modeling of bacterial internalization into osteoblastic cells was
supported. However, the concept of the competition between native
cells and bacteria, that is, “race for the surface, ” was felt to be limited
and that the many variables made the concept of a race inadequate.
Overall, there was 58% agreement and 31% disagreement.
The presence of persisters and SCV may further drive the need
for benchmarks. While it is clear that persisters are clinically
important, the role of SCV is not clear. There were also some
unanswered questions dealing with the acquisition of antibiotic
resistance and altered virulence, which are compelling subjects
requiring further exploration.
Overall, the questions addressed in this section highlighted the
need for studies that include both in vitro determinations with in vivo
outcomes, and the need for better characterization of the different
variables that determine pathogen behavior.
AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
All authors participated in data generation (identification of the
research questions and voting on their priority), contributed to the
writing, and have read and approved the final submitted manuscript.

__data__.text:
Correspondence
Jessica Amber Jennings, Department of
Biomedical Engineering, University of
Memphis, Memphis, TN, USA.
Email: jjnnings@memphis.edu
Abstract
In vitro and in vivo studies are critical for t he preclinical efficacy assessment of novel
therapies targeting musculoskeletal infections (MSKI). Many pre clinical models have
been developed and applied as a prelude t o evaluating safety and efficacy in human
clinical trials. In performin g these studies, there is both a requirement for a robust
assessment of efficacy, as well as a parallel responsibility to consider the burden on
experimental animals used in such studies. Since MSKI is a broad term encompassing
infections varying in pathogen, anatomical location, and implants used, there are also a
wide range of animal models described modeling these disparate infections. Although
some of these variations are required to adequately evaluate specific interventions,
there would be enormous value in creating a unified and standardized criteria to animal
testing in the treatment of MSKI. The Treatment Workgroup of the 2023 International
Consensus Meeting on Musculoskeletal Infection was responsible for questions related
to preclinical models for treatment of MSKI. The main objective was to review the
literature related to priority questions and estimate consensus opinion after voting. This
document presents that process and results for preclinical models related to (1) animal
model considerations, (2) outcome measurements, and (3) imaging.
KEYWORDS
antibiotic, biomarkers, fracture, imaging, infection
1 | INTRODUCTION
Musculoskeletal infections (MSKI) include infections of the bone,
muscles, or joints and encompass periprosthetic joint infection (PJI),
fracture‐related infection (FRI), septic arthritis, vertebral osteo-
myelitis as well as native osteomyelitis. Even with optimal and
appropriate preventative measures, MSKI occurs at a rate of 1% – 2%
for prosthetic joint replacements and 5% – 30% for traumatic injuries
such as open fractures.
1 Infection rates are even higher for military
cohorts and may compromise 63% of nonbattle related injuries and
nearly 90% of injuries among deployed service members. 2,3
Once established, MSKI are a devastating complication,
requiring (re)hospitalization, r epeated surgery, increased out ‐
patient visits, and lead to bone defects requiring prolonged
reconstruction, functional impairments, and reduced quality of
life
4,5 The treatment of established MSKI is generally centered
around surgical debridement and antibiotic therapy. Current
treatment options are built around surgical interventions, being
either implant retention or removal, or one or two ‐stage exchange,
with the presence and presumed maturity of the biofilm being a
major deciding factor. Despite the availability of algorithms aiding
in selecting the optimal treatment approach, treatment success
rates for MSKI vary widely and are often poorest in cases where
such algorithms cannot be followed. The consequences of failed
therapy include infection recur rence, progression to chronic
infection, and potentially the c reation of large bone defects due
to excision of infected tissues. These factors all drive substantially
increased patient morbidity and healthcare costs.
The clinical and economic implications of MSKI have driven
multiple lines of research into novel antimicrobials, antimicrobial ‐
laden bone void fillers and wound management strategies to increase
treatment success rates. Research and development exploring and
evaluating innovations in the treatment of MSKI require preclinical
models capable of mimicking and monitoring the development and
progression of infection, and its response to therapy. Although MSKI
is regularly studied in animal models, there seems to be little if any
agreement on which model to use in preclinical testing, with
individual institutions often establishing and using their own model
with little transfer across the field. The lack of consistency in
methodology can contribute to difficulty in comparing results
between studies, failure to use best practices in all aspects of study
execution (e.g., animal welfare, infection induction and treatment,
outcome measures etc.). In 2023, an international consensus meeting
(ICM) focused on preclinical models of MSKI was held at the
Orthopaedic Research Society (ORS) Annual Meeting to establish
expert recommendations for the use and evaluation of preclinical
infection research. The workgroup was established to focus on issues
related to animal models studying infection treatment.

__data__.text:
Although MSKI
is regularly studied in animal models, there seems to be little if any
agreement on which model to use in preclinical testing, with
individual institutions often establishing and using their own model
with little transfer across the field. The lack of consistency in
methodology can contribute to difficulty in comparing results
between studies, failure to use best practices in all aspects of study
execution (e.g., animal welfare, infection induction and treatment,
outcome measures etc.). In 2023, an international consensus meeting
(ICM) focused on preclinical models of MSKI was held at the
Orthopaedic Research Society (ORS) Annual Meeting to establish
expert recommendations for the use and evaluation of preclinical
infection research. The workgroup was established to focus on issues
related to animal models studying infection treatment. Led by a panel
of three co ‐chairs, the working group identified key questions,
assigned to a wider panel of delegates and co ‐authors, and managed
the literature review, preparation of summary statements, and
assembled the vote on recommendations. This consensus article

__data__.text:
provides these responses in addition to commentary on future
research goals arising from this initiative.
2 | METHODOLOGY
The main objective of ICM 2023 was to collect and assemble the best
available data on preclinical models of MSKI with input from experts
around the world to formulate a consensus document ( https://ors-
org.s3.amazonaws.com/wp-cont ent/uploads/2023/06/ORS-MSKI-
RIG-ICM-Final.pdf). Preclinical models are used to evaluate treatment
outcomes for novel therapeutics or protocols and thus directly
impacts patients impacted by implant ‐related, bone and joint
infections (MSKI), including surgical site infections, PJI, and FRI.
The first step, led by ICM 2023 cochairs, Drs. Edward Schwarz,
Fintan Moriarty, Antonia Chen, Tom Schaer, Kordo Saeed, and
Noreen Hickok were to identify and recruit an international panel of
MSKI experts. The experts served as delegates tasked to identify the
common practices and challenges related to preclinical models of
MSKI, then seek consensus on those issues using Delphi methodol-
ogy
6 to achieve “general agreement ” in response to questions.
Delegates with well ‐founded expertise in the field of MSKI
were invited and generated questions addressing clinical issues
related to MSKI. Questions were categorized into research areas,
each addressed by separate work groups, including a workgroup to
address issues related to treatment. Over 24 months, each
question was assigned to two or more delegates for response
preparation. Response preparation consisted of a literature review,
data summary, and an independent narrative response written
from the perspective and experience of each authoring delegate.
Responses were reviewed by facilitators and compiled in a
document. The compiled responses were then edited by all
authors to an agreed response to be distributed to all delegates
for review and comment. The authoring delegates refined their
responses based on the comments in preparation for discussion
and voting at the in ‐person consensus meeting held on February
10, 2023, in Dallas, TX, USA.
The controversial questions and responses were further discussed
and edited for online voting. The delegates then voted to (1) agree; (2)
disagree; or (3) abstain, on each, following Delphi methodology
7 and the
voting results were rated as (a) Simple majority (50.1% – 59%); No
Consensus; (b) Majority (60%– 6 5 % ) ;W e a kC o n s e n s u s ;( c )S u p e rM a j o r i t y
(66%– 99%); Strong Consensus; and (d) Unanimous (100%): Unanimous
Consensus. Among the work groups, there was one made up of the 33
authors in this consensus document dedicated to treatment. This group
consisted of infection experts from biomedical engineering, immunology,
infectious diseases, microbiology,and orthopaedic surgery, among other
disciplines. The treatment workgroup was tasked with discussion and
edits of the 12 treatment ‐related questions that were considered
relevant for preclinical research. The Workgroup emphasizes that
consensus was reached without comp ulsion, undue influential power,
inability to comprehend another course of action, or impatience with the
process of discussion and voting. Below is a summary of the 12
treatment‐related questions, responses, and/or recommendations, with
HTML links to a downloadable PDFs for each question, response,
consensus, and postmeeting rationale ( https://ors-org.s3.amazonaws.
com/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/ORS-MSKI-RIG-ICM-Final.pdf)a n d
the ICM voting results (https://ors-org.s3.amazonaws.com/wp-content/
uploads/2023/06/ORS-MSKI-ICM-Vote.pdf)a r ea v a i l a b l eo nt h eO R S
website.
3 | RESULTS
The Treatment Workgroup's responses to the 12 questions are
summarized in Table 1, which covers questions related to infection
model and inoculation, assessment of treatment efficacy, and
complementary imaging methods for evaluation of tissue response.
The strength of evidence assigned to each response is based on the
quality of studies and scientific validity of data and agreed upon by
members of the workgroup.

the ICM voting results (https://ors-org.s3.amazonaws.com/wp-content/
uploads/2023/06/ORS-MSKI-ICM-Vote.pdf)a r ea v a i l a b l eo nt h eO R S
website.
3 | RESULTS
The Treatment Workgroup's responses to the 12 questions are
summarized in Table 1, which covers questions related to infection
model and inoculation, assessment of treatment efficacy, and
complementary imaging methods for evaluation of tissue response.
The strength of evidence assigned to each response is based on the
quality of studies and scientific validity of data and agreed upon by
members of the workgroup. There were equal numbers of Strong and
Moderate Level evidence for treatment ‐based questions, with two
answers having responses based on low or limited evidence from
reported literature. Here we present an interpretive discussion of the
responses, grouped according to three topics of animal model
considerations, outcome measurements, and imaging. The readership
is encouraged to access the full rationale for each question in the ICM
2023 document ( https://ors-org.s3.amazonaws.com/wp-content/
uploads/2023/06/ORS-MSKI-RIG-ICM-Final.pdf) to gain a deeper
understanding of the available data.
3.1 | Animal model considerations
Six questions were related to details on the animal model considera-
tions, including duration, availability of models with specific
treatment modalities (surgical and antibiotic ‐related), bacterial
inoculum, and antibiotic selection.
The first question related to the timeline for observation
posttreatment to determine efficacy of treatment. The answer
provided by expert review stated “No,” and a super majority agreed
that there was not a standard timeline to best evaluate the resolution
of infection in animal models of MSKI. In response to Question 16,
delegates voted 88.9% in agreement, 5.6% in disagreement, and 5.6%
abstained. The answer was motivated by the fact that in any study,
the results will depend on multiple variables: infectious agent, the
extent of bone infection, anatomic location, and/or the animal
species used. This situation also complicates human clinical scenarios
of infection. The recommendation called for using clinical paradigms
to guide model selection, driven by considerations of the clinical
scenario being modeled, inoculum type, bone remodeling/response,
and current recommended practices; there are many scenarios that
accompany MSKI. While true, it is also fair to say that specific injuries
or pathogens are more common in MSKI. From these more prevalent
scenarios, a timeline may be established on a case ‐specific basis. Still,
overall, clinical guidelines for managing infection lack a clear

__data__.text:
TABLE 1 Treatment workgroup ‐related questions, responses, and level of agreement by ICM delegates.
Question 16 Is there a standard timeline or duration of treatment to best evaluate resolution of infection in animal models of MSKI?
Answer No
Rationale Timelines for studying infection resolution in animal models of osteomyelitis, fracture ‐related infection, and PJI are highly dependent
on the model, i.e., infectious agent, the extent of bone infection, anatomical location, and the species of animal used. Even in
human clinical scenarios, these timelines are highly variable from patient to patient under similar circumstances. Currently, there is
no definitive evidence to indicate the optimal timelines of infection treatment for in vivo models of osteomyelitis, facture ‐related
infection, and PJI. We recommend using existing clinical paradigms to guide model selection and study timelines. This is guided by
four considerations: clinical scenario being modeled, inoculum type, bone remodeling/response, and current recommended
practices.
Strength Moderate
Vote Agree: 89, Disagree: 6, Abstain: 5
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 17 Does radiography score correlate to infection and treatment efficacy for bone?
Answer Yes
Rationale Radiographic scoring strongly correlates to bone infection when X ‐ray imaging is performed 1 – 2 weeks or greater following infection.
Radiographic scoring also strongly correlates to treatment efficacy for bone infection when X ‐ray imaging is performed following
substantial differences in infection burden between groups over a duration of approximately 3 weeks or greater. Limitations in
radiographic scoring include the inability to detect differences in early infection and quantify early treatment efficacy. Limitations
in current literature assessment include variability in radiographic scoring methodology and animal model used.
Strength Moderate
Vote Agree: 82, Disagree: 8, Abstain: 10
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 18 Are there any imaging techniques available that can effectively indicate the degree of infection or monitor the advancement of the
disease in animal models of MSKI?
Answer Yes
Rationale Yes
There are imaging techniques available that can effectively indicate the degree of infection or monitor the advancement of the
disease in animal models of MSKI. X ‐ray can provide basic assessment of trabecular and cortical bone structure changes over
longitudinal assessment during the development of MSKI. Micro ‐CT is valuable for revealing microarchitecture of periosteal
reaction, medullary low ‐attenuation areas, trabecular coarsening, and focal cortical erosions with high precision in small animal
models. However, both X ‐ray and micro‐CT are limited in detecting features of disease progression often days to a week after the
initiation of infection and treatment. MRI is more sensitive to the presence of soft ‐tissue abnormalities and acute osteomyelitis
than X ‐ray and CT due to its in ‐depth determination of tissue swelling or fluid formation, formation of ducti, evaluation of soft
tissue involvement in infection, and assessment of angiogenesis. PET/CT shows promise in detecting infection boundaries and
likely has the potential to define the scope of lesions more accurately. Additionally, PET/CT can calculate blood flow through a
specific area of interest in real time. Finally, some novel imaging techniques, such as nano ‐CT, are expected to improve the
detection of MKSI advancement in in vivo models.
Strength Strong
Vote Agree: 92, Disagree: 3, Abstain: 5
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 19 Are IVIS using fluorescence or luminescence complementary to other methods such as culture or PCR?
Answer Yes
Rationale Yes
We conclude that IVIS provides complementary information on the bacterial burden to culture and PCR, and its usage is particularly
valuable in small animal models for in vivo longitudinal assessments. Limitations of IVIS, compared to CFU analysis or PCR,
include less reliable detection and quantification of infection at low bacterial concentrations and infections localized deep
underneath tissue. Furthermore, since the production of bioluminescent signals relies on metabolically active bacteria, data
obtained by IVIS are less reliable when metabolic activity is decreased (such as when biofilm is present). This should be taken into
consideration in the design of MSKI studies.
Strength Strong
Vote Agree: 93, Disagree: 4, Abstain: 3
(Continues)
JENNINGS ET AL .

__data__.text:
TABLE 1 (Continued)
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 20 Can techniques be employed to precisely evaluate the formation of biofilms on implants or infected bone in in vivo models of
osteomyelitis?
Answer Yes
Rationale Yes
Biofilm formation in vivo differs structurally from in vitro biofilm, as the latter lacks the mushroom ‐like structure and is smaller in size
1. In vivo models of implant ‐related bone infection demonstrate complex interactions between the host, the implants and
microbes, leading to milieu bacterial adhesion.Various techniques are available to assess the formation of biofilms on implants or
infected bone in vivo models of osteomyelitis. These include microscopic analysis, biomolecular assays, BLI, X ‐ray imaging, and
histological analysis. Microscopic analysis, such as SEM, FE ‐SEM, and CLSM, allows for detailed visualization and characterization
of biofilm structure and composition. Biomolecular assays, like ELISAs, enable the detection and quantification of specific
molecules associated with biofilm formation. BLI provides real ‐time monitoring of biofilm formation using genetically engineered
bacteria that emit light under specific conditions. X ‐ray imaging, specifically micro ‐CT, allows for visualization of biofilm
distribution on implants or infected bone. The histological analysis enables visualization of infection extent and biofilm formation
in surrounding tissue. The selection of technique(s) depends on the research question and the type of implant or infected bone
being studied. Combining multiple techniques can provide a more comprehensive understanding of biofilm formation in vivo.
Strength Strong
Vote Agree: 89, Disagree: 1, Abstain: 10
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 21 Is there an animal model representative of DAIR?
Answer Yes
Rationale Much has been published on PJI and DAIR models in rodents and rabbits; however, little information is available on DAIR models for
other animals. Few PJI models contain implants materially similar to those used clinically. Further, the amount and strain of
inoculation vastly differ between studies, which makes a determination of an appropriate DAIR intervention timepoint difficult.
Additional work should include implant resemblance, inoculation technique, and time to DAIR intervention as considerations.
Strength Moderate
Vote Agree: 83, Disagree: 6, Abstain: 11
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 22 Is there a single predominant combination of bacterial species in humans that should be studied in animal models of
polymicrobial MSKI?
Answer No
Rationale Polymicrobial infections do not have a particular combination of pathogens and are rare in the context of MSKIs. While no particular strain is
responsible for such infections, gram‐negative bacteria may be more common in polymicrobial infections. The incidence of polymicrobial
infections depends on the type and location of the infection (osteomyelitis, fracture infections, spondylodiscitis, etc.), and are caused by
various strains of bacteria. These strains include Gram‐negative bacilli, nonepidermidis coagulase‐negative Staphylococci,Staphylococcus
epidermidis,a n d Staphylococcus aureus. Animal models of polymicrobial infections are limited in literature and have difficulties like
determining the optimal bacterial inoculum. Therefore, polymicrobial infections in animal models should be aimed at answering specific
research questions (synergism, interactions, etc.), and may contain gram‐negative strains.
Strength Low
Vote Agree: 85,
Disagree: 7, Abstain: 8
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 23 Are there immunological plasma biomarkers that are useful to measure infection or treatment effects in rat models of MSKI?
Answer Yes
Rationale There are markers which have demonstrated efficacy in longitudinal rat inflammation and infection studies. Haptoglobin, α2M,
fibrinogen, TNF ‐α, and CRP have demonstrated correlation with infection and inflammation in rats shortly (~1 – 7 days) after
infection, with or without injury. Data on these markers as indicators of treatment effect are limited, with a few studies reporting
utility of haptoglobin. Due to varying pathogeneses of infectious agents and levels of inflammation, there is likely not a single
superior marker for tracking infection or treatment efficacy in rats. Recent studies analyzing multiple plasma markers and cell
types have begun to enable a more complete understanding of the immune response to infection.

__data__.text:
TABLE 1 (Continued)
Vote Agree: 86, Disagree: 6, Abstain: 8
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 24 Can all SOC antibiotics included in the clinical treatment guidelines for MSKI be used in animal models?
Answer Yes
Rationale All SOC antibiotics can be used in animal models of MSKI. The optimal choice of antibiotic therapy relies on a variety of criteria
including the animal involved, the presence of a foreign body, causative agent, the presence of a biofilm, and the aim of the study.
All first ‐line antibiotics have been utilized in animal models of MSKI, which frequently continues for multiple weeks or until the
causal agent is identified. This demonstrates that antibiotic treatment using SOC drugs is feasible in animals.
Strength Strong
Vote Agree: 90, Disagree: 3, Abstain: 7
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 25 Consolidated with question 24
Question 26 Is a statistically significant reduction in bacterial burden clinically significant if infection remains after treatment in animal mod els of
infection?
Answer Yes
Rationale The literature linking changes in CFU counts in animal models with clinically significant changes in infection is weak and
circumstantial. Despite these weaknesses, there does appear to be some evidence that bacterial loads generated in current in vivo
models does replicate findings from clinical studies in the chronic wound literature in humans. Additionally, thresholds below
103– 104 CFU, which would correlate in most studies to a 2 ‐log reduction in bacterial load would be below the threshold that has
been used clinically to roughly define appropriate wound healing and skin colonization versus active infection in chronic wound
healing studies in humans. This would probably be a minimum reasonable threshold to use for in vivo studies.
Strength Limited
Vote Agree: 69, Disagree: 18, Abstain: 13
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 27 After using an antimicrobial ‐loaded biomaterial in an animal study, can you prevent false negative culture results due to
antimicrobial carryover during sample processing in the lab?
Answer Yes
Rationale False negative cultures occur when study antibiotics or antiseptics kill bacteria or inhibit culture after the time of sampling. In the cas e
of antibiotics, super ‐MIC antibiotic concentrations accompany bacteria into culture plates. To this end, preventing false negatives
requires one of several techniques to either dilute or remove antibiotics such that antibiotic concentrations are below MIC values
when culturing, e.g., pelleting/washing or neutralization in combination with good laboratory practices and controls.
Strength Strong
Vote Agree: 90, Disagree: 4, Abstain: 6
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Question 28 Should treatment of fracture and/or implant ‐related infection always include debridement in animal models?
Answer Yes
Rationale Debridement should always be considered as part of the SOC for treating fracture and/or implant ‐related infections in animal models.
Strength Moderate
Vote Agree: 71, Disagree: 18, Abstain: 11
Result Super Majority, Strong Consensus
Abbreviations: α2M, alpha2 ‐macroglobulin; BLI, bioluminescence imaging; CFU, colony forming unit; CLSM, confocal laser scanning microscopy; CRP,
c‐reactive protein; CT, computed tomography; DAIR, debridement, antibiotics, and implant retention; ELISA, enzyme ‐linked immunosorbent assay;
FE‐SEM, field ‐emission scanning electron microscopy; ICM, international consensus meeting; IVIS, in vivo imaging systems; MIC, minimum inhibitory
concentration; MRI, magnetic resonance imaging; MSKI, musculoskeletal infections; PCR, polymerase chain reactio; PET, positron emission tomogr aphy;
PJI, periprosthetic joint infection; SEM, scanning electron microscopy; SOC, standard of care; TNF ‐α, tumor necrosis factor ‐alpha.

__data__.text:
consensus. Thus, it is challenging to offer a standard timeline in an
animal model, especially considering that human cases of chronic
infection may extend from weeks to months. The creation of a
definitive timeline for infection resolution in animal models of MSKI
must start with specific considerations related to the condition of
interest and may best be performed for the most common pathogens
for defined infection types.
In response to Question 21 about whether animal models of
drbridement, antibiotics, and implant retention (DAIR) are available.
The recommendation response was, “Yes, there have been several
animal models for DAIR, ” with no perceived controversy. Delegates
voted 83.3% in agreement, 5.6% in disagreement, and 11.1%
abstained. Much has been published on PJI and DAIR models in
rodents and rabbits. However, little information is available on DAIR
models for other animals.
8– 12 While there is no ideal model or one
that very closely replicates clinical scenarios, moderate evidence
supports that modest DAIR models exist. Few PJI models contain
implants similar to those used clinically. Further, there is a wide
variety between studies in the amount and strain of inoculationmakes
determination of an appropriate DAIR intervention timepoint diffi-
cult. Future work is necessary to develop more clinically relevant
models that allow for infection to develop, DAIR to be performed in a
meaningful manner, and allow timeframes of healing to occur with
outcome measures modeling clinical practice, for example, white
blood cell counts, fever, swelling, erythrocyte sedimentation rate,
cytokine analysis, and other metrics as deemed relevant to the clinical
scenario being modeled. Additional work should include implant
functionality, inoculation technique, and time to DAIR intervention as
considerations.
Question 22 addresses polymicrobial infections in animal models
of MSKIs and how these models should be designed. A super
majority, 85% agreed with the response of “No” to the question of
whether there is a single predominant combination of bacterial
species in humans that should be studied in animal models of
polymicrobial MSKI, 7% disagreed, and 8% abstained. Depending on
the etiology (osteomyelitis, PJI, FRI, spondylodiscitis, and infected
nonunions) and local epidemiology, the prevalence of polymicrobial
infections in humans ranges from about 10% to 25% associated with
a wide range of pathogens with no particular combination dominat-
ing.
13– 15 About 65% of these infection cases are caused by Gram ‐
negative bacilli, followed by nonepidermidis coagulase ‐negative
Staphylococci, Staphylococcus epidermidis , and Staphylococcus
aureus. In polymicrobial infections, a complex environment may be
formed, in which microbiological synergistic interactions exist
between microorganisms. This microbial synergism, in line with the
need for broad ‐spectrum antimicrobial therapy, may result in a more
challenging treatment, but also adverse outcomes for patients
suffering from MSKIs.
16,17 Limited literature exists on polymicrobial
infections of the musculoskeletal system in animal models. Available
models include polymicrobial infections induced by S. aureus in
combination with Pseudomonas aeruginosa , while one model instead
examines co ‐infection with Escherichia coli .18– 21 These models have
encountered challenges in establishing appropriate bacterial
inoculum concentrations to induce infection without compromising
animal welfare. Nevertheless, no serious complications or high rates
of animal exclusion have been reported in these studies.
18– 21
Therefore, polymicrobial infections in animal models should aim to
answer specific research questions (synergism, interactions, etc.), and
may contain Gram ‐negative strains. In conclusion, animal models for
polymicrobial MSKIs should be limited to specific research questions
such as pathogen interactions or specific treatment approaches. If
such a model is planned, it may contain at least one Gram ‐negative
pathogen, consistent with the microbial etiology in MSKIs.
Question 24 addresses the use of standard ‐of‐care (SOC)
antibiotics in animal models of MSKI.

metadata.file_path: E:\Guideline-driven-AI\backend\..\guidelines\Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Jennings - 2023 International Consensus Meeting on musculoskeletal infection  Summary.pdf

__data__.text:
Nevertheless, no serious complications or high rates
of animal exclusion have been reported in these studies.
18– 21
Therefore, polymicrobial infections in animal models should aim to
answer specific research questions (synergism, interactions, etc.), and
may contain Gram ‐negative strains. In conclusion, animal models for
polymicrobial MSKIs should be limited to specific research questions
such as pathogen interactions or specific treatment approaches. If
such a model is planned, it may contain at least one Gram ‐negative
pathogen, consistent with the microbial etiology in MSKIs.
Question 24 addresses the use of standard ‐of‐care (SOC)
antibiotics in animal models of MSKI. Specifically, the question
asked, “Can all standard ‐of‐care antibiotics included in the clinical
treatment guidelines for musculoskeletal infection be used in animal
models?” Delegates voted 90% stating, “Yes,” the SOC antibiotics
used in clinical treatments can be included in the clinical guidelines
for MSKI in animal models, 3% voted in disagreement, and 7%
abstained. The optimal choice of antibiotic therapy relies on a variety
of criteria including the animal involved, presence of a foreign body,
causative agent, presence of a biofilm, and the aim of the study. In
human patients, treatment is often started empirically with broad ‐
spectrum antibiotics and switched to a more targeted approach when
the causative pathogen is known. Clinical guidelines are developed to
advise the optimal treatment strategy based on the pathogen,
antibiotic resistance patterns, and the presence of foreign materials
(e.g., prosthesis or osteosynthesis material). Understandably, the
preclinical studies that were found to address question 24 focused on
the most frequently encountered bacteria in MSKIs (e.g., Staphylo-
coccal species, Enterobacteriaceae, and P. aeruginosa ), and so
antibiotic therapies described in the literature are most often
targeting these pathogens, with more unusual pathogens rarely, if
ever, studied. The systematic review of literature confirmed that all
antibiotics, recommended by the clinical guidelines to treat infections
with the most common pathogens, have been administered previ-
ously in animal models. Data was collected on the administration
route, the total daily dose, and the duration of antibiotic administra-
tion. However, whether the use of using such a large variety of
antimicrobials in preclinical studies is favorable over a more
standardized approach was not assessed during the consensus
meeting. The need for using a specific antibiotic is obvious with
certain research questions, like evaluating bioavailability or biofilm
activity of a particular drug. When the study does not directly aim to
evaluate the systemic antibiotic itself, it might be preferable to
pursue a more standardized approach and use only first ‐line
antibiotics of the clinical guidelines. This would reduce the range of
antibiotics tested and improve consistency between different studies
and limit the effect of a difference in systemic antibiotic regimens on
the study outcome. The development of a more regulated approach
would require additional information on translating antibiotic effects
from animals to humans, optimal dosing strategies, and the safety and
tolerability of different treatment strategies.
Fracture and/or implant ‐related infection has a high morbidity
burden in orthopaedic patients and a prompt diagnosis is an important

metadata.file_path: E:\Guideline-driven-AI\backend\..\guidelines\Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Jennings - 2023 International Consensus Meeting on musculoskeletal infection  Summary.pdf

__data__.text:
step in the treatment process. Question 28 asked, “Should treatment
of fracture and/or implant ‐related infections always include debride-
ment in animal models?. ” The delegate votes were 71% in agreement,
18% disagreement, and 11% abstained for the recommendation of
“yes.” Diagnostic criteria suggested by Metsemakers et al. 22 and
Govaert et al. 23 concluded presence of sinus, fistula, or wound
breakdown (with communication to the bone or implant) and/or
purulent drainage from the wound or presence of pus during surgery
and these are pathognomonic for the diagnosis of FRI.
22,23 Any other
clinical sign of infection, apart from the aforementioned, are
considered suggestive and should prompt further investigation from
physician or surgeon. If there is presence of any of the confirmatory
criteria, debridement should be considered as part of SOC since it
helps remove necrotic tissue and biofilm matrix, reduces bacterial
count, and prevents progression of biofilm formation. The consensus
voting results for the question, “Should treatment of fracture and/or
implant‐related infection always include debridement in animal
models?” was one of the lowest agreements in the process at 70%.
Although speculative, reasons behind this may be due to the practical
issues of repeated surgery and the technical expertise required not
being available in many basic research settings. However, considering
the central role played by debridement in the clinic, it seems logical to
include it in animal studies whenever appropriate or possible. Any
treatment strategy that successfully treats MSKI in the absence of
debridement would of course be a powerful estimation of efficacy.
3.2 | Outcome measurements
Question 20 investigates if techniques be employed to precisely
evaluate the formation of biofilms on implants or infected bone in in
vivo models of osteomyelitis. Delegates voted 89% in agreement, 1%
disagreed that “Yes, techniques can be employed to precisely evaluate
the formation of biofilms on implants or infected bone in in vivo models
of osteomyelitis” and 10% abstained. The high number of abstentions
indicates that many may have been confused about the meaning of the
answer and its relation to preclinical models of treatment or did not feel
qualified to answer the question. Microscopy (histology) is routinely
used in clinics for diagnosing and evaluating infection of the bone and
can be utilized as a viable approach for probing biofilms formation on
implants in labs with specialized equipment. While these are routine,
they are repeatedly modified and there is no standard technique for
imaging and analysis at a high resolution. Scanning electron micros-
copy is a standard technique used for studying the surface morphology;
however, the technique cannot examine qualitative and quantitative
information on the biofilm structure at high resolutions.
24 It is generally
implicit that biofilm on implants should be detected, but there may be
disagreement on the level of precision current techniques offer.
Advancements in biofilm detection and measurement techniques are
needed to improve the rigor of preclinical models evaluating
treatments.
Question 23 assesses whether any biomarkers may be useful as
indicators of infection or treatment effects in rat models of MSKI.
Delegates voted 86.1% in agreement, 5.6% in disagreement, and
8.3% abstained for the response of “Yes” markers demonstrating
efficacy are available. The high number of abstained voters may
correlate with the varying pathogeneses of infectious agents and
levels of inflammation making broad statements challenging. Clini-
cally, blood markers are used for diagnoses of infection in humans
(e.g., elevated white blood cell counts, C ‐reactive protein [CRP]).
25
Notably, biomarkers useful in humans are not necessarily effective
measures in rats (or other species) due to interspecies variability in
levels of response and baseline levels. This warrants investigation of
species‐specific markers which effectively elucidate infection and/or
treatment effects. Plasma biomarkers participate in complex signaling
pathways, and their expression changes over time following infection
and/or inflammation.

__data__.text:
Delegates voted 86.1% in agreement, 5.6% in disagreement, and
8.3% abstained for the response of “Yes” markers demonstrating
efficacy are available. The high number of abstained voters may
correlate with the varying pathogeneses of infectious agents and
levels of inflammation making broad statements challenging. Clini-
cally, blood markers are used for diagnoses of infection in humans
(e.g., elevated white blood cell counts, C ‐reactive protein [CRP]).
25
Notably, biomarkers useful in humans are not necessarily effective
measures in rats (or other species) due to interspecies variability in
levels of response and baseline levels. This warrants investigation of
species‐specific markers which effectively elucidate infection and/or
treatment effects. Plasma biomarkers participate in complex signaling
pathways, and their expression changes over time following infection
and/or inflammation. While haptoglobin, alpha
2‐macroglobulin,
fibrinogen, tumor necrosis factor ‐alpha, and CRP have demonstrated
correlation with infection and inflammation in rats shortly
(~1– 7 days) after infection, with or without injury, data on these
markers as indicators of treatment effect are limited. 26 The lack of
unanimous consensus may be due to this discrepancy. Further, as
different pathogens modulate the immune response through various
mechanisms which may alter bacterial load and mask treatment
effect,
27 more data is needed to confirm each biomarker's effective-
ness in distinguishing between infection models, and between
treated and untreated groups within an infection model. Methods
for quantifying these biomarkers include electrophoresis, enzyme ‐
linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs), and multiplex assays, but their
use is inconsistent across studies, which makes interpreting results
challenging. Electrophoresis and gel staining can distinguish multiple
molecules by size from the same sample. ELISAs are highly accurate
and sensitive but are generally designed for singular proteins or
macromolecules, requiring large volumes of blood to quantify
multiple markers. Multiple markers can be analyzed from the same
sample simultaneously in bead ‐based multiplex arrays, but these
require specialized instrumentation and can be less sensitive than
single‐factor ELISAs.
28 In some studies, statistical analyses were not
performed on both blood markers and traditional outcome measures
(i.e., colony forming unit [CFU] counts). In other studies, blood
markers and traditional outcome measures were statistically ana-
lyzed, but effects of infection and/or treatment were not detected. A
standardized, multiplex assay may be the most effective measure of
immune response across models, though such a panel remains to be
provided and represents an important future research target.
Question 26 addresses whether a statistically significant
decrease in bacterial burden in animal models of infection is clinically
significant. This is a challenging question because it is difficult to
directly translate findings in an animal model to the human setting.
The expert panel provided an answer of “Yes” although it was a
qualified statement with limited strength of recommendation. The
literature available to answer this question is mostly circumstantial
and may not be directly clinically applicable. As a result, this was a
controversial question among the delegates with only 69% in
agreement, 18% disagreement, and 12.5% abstaining. Typical animal
models use a 2 ‐ to 3 ‐log
10 reduction in bacterial burden with

__data__.text:
thresholds below 10 3– 104 CFU to define “successful” treatment of
underlying infection in experimental settings, and the thresholds of
bacterial burden necessary to generate infection appear to be in a
similar range.
29– 33 Unfortunately, whether these findings directly
translate to human infection is not clear. Some of the controversy lies
in the lack of data regarding bacterial burden in human infection as it
resolves with treatment. For instance, the thresholds for infection
eradication in clinical osteomyelitis are not known due to the
difficulty and lack of precise analysis at the infection site during
treatment. As a result, microbial cultures are relied upon for this
diagnosis, but are unreliable for several previously described reasons.
Most of the literature in humans where bacterial burden was
carefully characterized derives from the chronic wound literature,
which may not directly translate to MSKI.
29,34– 43 Nevertheless, in
human wound healing, minimum intrawound bacterial loads of
≥10
4 CFU/g tissue were needed to cause infection of complex
extremity wounds and postsurgical wounds, and this also appeared to
be the approximate threshold for successful skin grafting, which is
consistent with bacterial burdens used in clinical animal models.
41– 43
Further studies on longitudinally assessing bacterial burden during
the healing process of skeletal infections would be valuable.
In response to Question 27, related to preventing false ‐negative
culture due to antimicrobial carryover, delegates voted 90.3% in
agreement, 4.2% in disagreement, and 5.6% abstained. The recom-
mendation response was, “Yes, false negative cultures occur when
study antibiotics or antiseptics kill bacteria or inhibit culture after the
time of sampling, ” with no perceived controversy. Various methods
exist to mitigate false negative culture results, primarily those that
dilute the antimicrobial to a level where they are below the minimum
inhibitory concentration and will not result in residual kill against
bacteria. Future techniques could be improved by regulatory
agencies or standardization organizations, for example, ISO or ATSM,
establishing standards that apply to a broad range of culture
techniques with specific intent to mitigate false culture negative
potential.
3.3 | Imaging
The majority (81.9%) of the voting delegates of the ORS
I C Ma g r e e dw i t ht h er e c o m m e n d a t i o ni nq u e s t i o n1 7t h a t
radiographic (X ‐ray) scoring correlates to infection and treatment
efficacy for bone. However, 8.3% disagreed with this recommen-
dation. Some of this disagreement could be due to limitations in
X‐ray detecting early and subtle changes with early infection or
early treatment. Furthermore, when X ‐ray scoring is utilized, there
is variability across studies in terms of X ‐ray scoring methodology,
animal model, and/or therapeutic a ssessed. These limitations are
reflected in our moderate strength of recommendation and may
have led to some disagreement across some of the ICM delegates.
While X ‐ray scoring can be a valuable tool for assessing bone
infection and treatment of bone infection, developing and
adopting consensus recommendations for X ‐ray scoring
methodology for respective animal models with bone infection
could enhance the generalizability of X ‐ray scoring across studies
and increase agreement across ICM delegates.
Questions 18 and 19 both involve imaging techniques such as in
vivo imaging systems (IVIS) and whether they can effectively monitor
the degree of infection. Delegates for question 18 voted 92% in
agreement, 3% disagreed, and 5% abstained. Both questions show
the strongest level of agreement among the questions. Low
disagreement confirms that there is little controversy about the
advantages of imaging techniques. Question 18 specifically targets
whether imaging techniques are available that can effectively indicate
the degree of infection or monitor the advancement of the disease in
animal models of MSKI. The majority of voters answered yes and
referenced X ‐rays as a basic assessment of trabecular and cortical
bone structure changes over longitudinal assessment during the
development of MSKI. The advantages and disadvantages of
microcomputed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging
(MRI) were also explored.

metadata.file_path: E:\Guideline-driven-AI\backend\..\guidelines\Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Jennings - 2023 International Consensus Meeting on musculoskeletal infection  Summary.pdf

__data__.text:
Questions 18 and 19 both involve imaging techniques such as in
vivo imaging systems (IVIS) and whether they can effectively monitor
the degree of infection. Delegates for question 18 voted 92% in
agreement, 3% disagreed, and 5% abstained. Both questions show
the strongest level of agreement among the questions. Low
disagreement confirms that there is little controversy about the
advantages of imaging techniques. Question 18 specifically targets
whether imaging techniques are available that can effectively indicate
the degree of infection or monitor the advancement of the disease in
animal models of MSKI. The majority of voters answered yes and
referenced X ‐rays as a basic assessment of trabecular and cortical
bone structure changes over longitudinal assessment during the
development of MSKI. The advantages and disadvantages of
microcomputed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging
(MRI) were also explored. Studies show CT as a valuable imaging
technique for revealing microarchitecture of periosteal reaction,
medullary low ‐attenuation areas, trabecular coarsening, and focal
cortical erosions with high precision in small animal models. However,
both X ‐ray and micro ‐CT are limited in detecting features of disease
progression often days to a week after the initiation of infection and
treatment. MRI is more sensitive to the presence of soft ‐tissue
abnormalities and acute osteomyelitis than X ‐ray and CT, due to its
in‐depth determination of tissue swelling or fluid formation, forma-
tion of ducti, evaluation of soft tissue involvement in infection, and
assessment of angiogenesis. Positron emission tomography (PET)/CT
shows promise in detecting infection boundaries and likely has the
potential to define the scope of lesions more accurately. Additionally,
PET/CT can calculate blood flow through a specific area of interest in
real time. Finally, some novel imaging techniques, such as nano ‐CT,
are expected to improve the detection of MKSI advancement in in
vivo models. Related to IVIS using fluorescence or luminescence, the
majority (93%) of delegates of the ORS ICM agreed with the
recommendation that IVIS imaging methods are complementary to
other methods such as culture or polymerase chain reaction (PCR).
This is the highest level of agreement that IVIS provides complemen-
tary information to culture or PCR regarding bacterial burden.
However, 4% of voters disagreed with this recommendation and
3% abstained. Some of this disagreement could be due to limitations
in IVIS imaging, such as quantification of infection at low bacterial
concentrations and infections localized deep underneath tissue. As
IVIS technology continues to advance, such as the development of
improved bioluminescent or fluorescent reporters, the limitations of
IVIS may be further diminished.
4 | DISCUSSION
The treatment working group of the ORS ICM tackled important
issues in the treatment of MSKI from different viewpoints. For
animal model considerations, a clear variety of models exist, and

metadata.file_name: Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Jennings - 2023 International Consensus Meeting on musculoskeletal infection  Summary.pdf
metadata.file_path: E:\Guideline-driven-AI\backend\..\guidelines\Journal Orthopaedic Research - 2023 - Jennings - 2023 International Consensus Meeting on musculoskeletal infection  Summary.pdf

__data__.text:
that all front ‐line antibiotics may be readily administered to
animals to closely replicate clini cal treatment options. This paves
the way for alignment on systemic an tibiotic controls across animal
models, which is not yet observed, but may be possible in future.
The absence of a standard timeline, which may reflect the need to
evaluate preclinical studies in simi lar contexts to clinical counter-
parts, does not have an obvious solution in the near term.
However, here it may be possible t o align protocols to aid cross
comparison of studies, but at the present time, it remains unclear
how to achieve this or convince research groups to deviate from
protocols that are su ccessful in the past to align with new
protocols. There were higher levels of disagreement or abstentions
for the recommendation that debridement always be included in
fracture ‐related infection models, which may be dependent on the
specific type of evaluation and the timing of intervention. The
number of abstentions for questions related to DAIR models and
polymicrobial models may reflect the lack of research on these
topics and unfamiliarity of experts with their use. More research is
needed to refine these models, and most recommendations agree
that these should be representative of clinical scenarios, while at
the same time, being perhaps mostly appropriate for certain
research questions.
There were high levels of agreement for consistency in
o u t c o m em e a s u r e ss u c ha st a k i n gm e a s u r e st op r e v e n tf a l s e‐
negative cultures due to antimicrobial carryover and methods to
evaluate biofilm on implants. Such practices are already described
and encouraged by journals. The availability of biomarkers in
preclinical models to measure treatment effects also had low levels
of disagreement among delegates, confirming their role as
complementary measures. Next steps for this topic include
broadening to other species and n ominating precise biomarker
assays for optimal monitoring of outcomes including response to
therapy. There were high levels of disagreement about the
statement that a statistically significant reduction of bacterial
burden is clinically significant, which may be related to the high
risk of rebound infection if bacteria and biofilm are not completely
eradicated at the site of injury. This topic is a good example of a
topic where consensus opinion is a good indicator of prevailing
thoughts on a topic, with no objec tive measure being available.
Therefore, subjective, expert ‐led opinion is a useful tool to guide
the field. The lack of consensus ind icates that further refinement
of this concept is required, and perhaps dedicate experimental
studies to provide evidence in support of a more definitive
statement in future.
Imaging remains an extremely useful tool in MSKI research but is
less quantitative than some outcome measures and in particular CFU
counting. Delegates showed strong support for the use of IVIS and
other imaging techniques as complementary methods, but also
highlighted their limitations. Use of radiographic scoring as an
outcome measure was less favored, likely because of the limitations
of the many scoring systems and lack of standardization of
evaluations. These methods provide valuable insight into the
infection status through minimally invasive methods with potential
to longitudinally monitor the progress of treatment or development
of infection. The consensus responses recommend these methods if
available but also caution about using them as the primary evaluation.
As such, they may be more reflective of boney changes secondary to
infection than the infection itself.
5 | CONCLUSION
The recommendations and voting results of the 2023 ICM may be
used to guide future research toward improved performance of
preclinical research on treating MSKI. Overall, there was majority
consensus for all the recommenda tions, although many responses
were based on limited or low ‐quality evidence found in the
literature and some questions had a high abstention rate. Future
research should be directed toward improving the quality of
evidence, particularly for questions of significance of reducing
bacterial burden but not eliminating it and the refinement of
polymicrobial models. Furtherm ore, the issue of the wide variety
of infections requiring a myriad of models is an issue that may
need some consolidation to improve the value of preclinical
research. Understanding and overcoming these issues will improve
research quality and in the long term will benefit patients and
clinicians.

__data__.text:
5 | CONCLUSION
The recommendations and voting results of the 2023 ICM may be
used to guide future research toward improved performance of
preclinical research on treating MSKI. Overall, there was majority
consensus for all the recommenda tions, although many responses
were based on limited or low ‐quality evidence found in the
literature and some questions had a high abstention rate. Future
research should be directed toward improving the quality of
evidence, particularly for questions of significance of reducing
bacterial burden but not eliminating it and the refinement of
polymicrobial models. Furtherm ore, the issue of the wide variety
of infections requiring a myriad of models is an issue that may
need some consolidation to improve the value of preclinical
research. Understanding and overcoming these issues will improve
research quality and in the long term will benefit patients and
clinicians.

__data__.text:
Received: 12 July 2023 | Revised: 18 October 2023 | Accepted: 28 November 2023
DOI: 10.1002/jor.25758
SPECIAL ISSUE ARTICLE
The 2023 Orthopaedic Research Society's International
Consensus Meeting on musculoskeletal infection:
Summary from the host immunity section
Edward M. Schwarz 1 | Nathan K. Archer 2 | Gerald J. Atkins 3 |
Karen L. de Mesy Bentley 4 | Mina Botros 1 | James E. Cassat 5 |
Emanuele Chisari 6 | Débora C. Coraça ‐Huber7 | John L. Daiss 1 |
Steven R. Gill 8 | Stuart B. Goodman 9 | Janette Harro 10 |
Christopher J. Hernandez 11 | Lionel B. Ivashkiv 12 | Stephen L. Kates 13 |
Cláudia N. H. Marques 14 | Elysia A. Masters 15 | Gowrishankar Muthukrishnan1 |
John R. Owen 13 | Dina Raafat 16,17 | Motoo Saito 1 | Deborah J. Veis 18 |
Chao Xie 1
Correspondence
Edward M. Schwarz, Department of
Orthopaedics, University of Rochester,
Rochester, NY, USA.
Email: Edward_Schwarz@urmc.rochester.edu
Abstract
Musculoskeletal infections (MSKI), which are a major problem in orthopedics, occur
when the pathogen eludes or overwhelms the host immune system. While effective
vaccines and immunotherapies to prevent and treat MSKI should be possible,
fundamental knowledge gaps in our understanding of protective, nonprotective, and
pathogenic host immunity are prohibitive. We also lack critical knowledge of how
host immunity is affected by the microbiome, implants, prior infection, nutrition,
antibiotics, and concomitant therapies, autoimmunity, and other comorbidities. To
define our current knowledge of these critical topics, a Host Immunity Section of the
2023 Orthopaedic Research Society MSKI International Consensus Meeting (ICM)
proposed 78 questions. Systematic reviews were performed on 15 of these
questions, upon which recommendations with level of evidence were voted on
by the 72 ICM delegates, and another 12 questions were voted on with a
recommendation of “Unknown” without systematic reviews. Two questions were
transferred to another ICM Section, and the other 45 were tabled for future
consideration due to limitations of available human resources. Here we report
the results of the voting with internet access to the questions, recommendations,
and rationale from the systematic reviews. Eighteen questions received a consensus
vote of ≥90%, while nine recommendations failed to achieve this threshold.
Commentary on why consensus was not achieved on these questions and potential
ways forward are provided to stimulate specific funding mechanisms and research
on these critical MSKI host defense questions.
J Orthop Res . 2024;42:518 – 530.wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/jor518 | © 2023 Orthopaedic Research Society.
For affiliations refer to page 528.

__data__.text:
KEYWORDS
host immunity, International Consensus Meeting, musculoskeletal infection (MSKI),
osteomyelitis, periprosthetic joint infection, surgical site infection
1 | INTRODUCTION AND BACKGROUND
Musculoskeletal infections (MSKI) continue to be the bane of orthopedic
surgery, as their poor clinical outcomes have great costs to patients and
healthcare systems. Most troubl ing is our inability to reduce the
approximately 1% infection rate following elective surgery,1– 4 even with
the most rigorous prophylaxis and aseptic surgical systems (e.g., outcomes
f r o mt h eS u r g i c a lC a r eI m p r o v e m e n tP r o j e c t( S C I P )2), suggesting that this
may be due to host factors. Moreover, reinfection rates and persistent
infection following revision surgery are still as high as 33%,
3,5,6 which also
suggests that these patients do not have a protective adaptive immune
response. It is also noteworthy that the major pathogens responsible for
MSKI are bacteria ( Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus agalactiae,a n d
Pseudomonas aeruginosa,e t c .
7) that cause serious infectious diseases in
other organ systems, and that all efforts to develop effective human
vaccines have failed.
8 Thus, major knowledge gaps in our fundamental
understanding of microbial pathogenesis and the host response to
virulence must be filled if we are to de velop effective interventions for
MSKI. Additionally, it has been ac knowledged that a major cause of
human vaccine failure is the lack of preclinical model systems that
faithfully replicate immune responses in real‐world clinical situations.9,10
Therefore, technical and conceptual innovations are needed.
Given that elucidation of all critical knowledge of MSKI cannot
be obtained from randomized controlled clinical trials (Level 1
evidence) due to lack of feasibility, overwhelming costs, and ethical
concerns, the orthopedic community turned to International Consen-
sus Meetings (ICM) to obtain the most current information and
establish priorities from expert opinions with its first ICM focused on
prosthetic joint infection (PJI) in 2013.
11 This meeting was a
tremendous success by several criteria including: (i) establishing an
initial precedent with a published description of the consensus
process;12 (ii) changing clinical practice with the first definition of PJI
that was formalized in 2018; 13 and (iii) establishing a body mass
index > 40 as a risk factor for PJI. 14 Moreover, this led to a second
ICM 5 years later that covered all aspects of MSKI (ICM 2018,
https://icmphilly.com). Importantly, two research workgroups con-
taining Orthopaedic Research Society (ORS) members produced
publications on the General Assembly questions,
14 and on the Biofilm
questions.15 There was also a third MSKI ICM in 2019 focused on
antibiotic‐loaded bone cement. 16
These initial ICM produced a new international community of
investigators that launched the ORS MSKI research interest group
(RIG) at the 2020 Annual Meeting in Austin, TX. At this time, the idea
of holding an ORS MSKI ICM focused on preclinical questions was
born, as animal modeling had largely been neglected in the prior
MSKI ICM, and the painful history of vaccine development without
careful attention to face, construct, and predictive value of the
preclinical models used was paramount.
17,18 Another strong motiva-
tion for this topic came from the ICM 2018 results, which determined
that of the General Assembly topics (microbiology/antimicrobials,
biomaterials/implants, surgery/clinical care, and immunology/host
immunity), “our knowledge of host immunity is by far the most
limited, and it is the only discipline that has not made a major
contribution to impact MSKI patients. ”
14 This was also reflected in
the ICM 2018 voting, in which only 62% of delegates agreed that
immunotherapy and immunoprophylaxis could be used to prevent
biofilm formation and implant ‐associated infections ( https://www.
ors.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/01/Question-5.pdf).

__data__.text:
17,18 Another strong motiva-
tion for this topic came from the ICM 2018 results, which determined
that of the General Assembly topics (microbiology/antimicrobials,
biomaterials/implants, surgery/clinical care, and immunology/host
immunity), “our knowledge of host immunity is by far the most
limited, and it is the only discipline that has not made a major
contribution to impact MSKI patients. ”
14 This was also reflected in
the ICM 2018 voting, in which only 62% of delegates agreed that
immunotherapy and immunoprophylaxis could be used to prevent
biofilm formation and implant ‐associated infections ( https://www.
ors.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/01/Question-5.pdf).
In recognition of the importance of MSKI immunology, the ICM
created a Host Immunity Section that originally drafted 78 questions.
Delegates volunteered to perform systematic reviews on up to three
of these questions. In the end, systematic reviews were performed on
only 15 of these questions, upon which recommendations with the
level of evidence were voted on by the 72 ICM delegates. Another 12
questions were voted on with a recommendation of “Unknown”
without systematic reviews, and the remainder were either trans-
ferred to another ICM Section or tabled due to the lack of human
resources for performing systematic reviews. Here we report the
results of the voting with internet access to the questions,
recommendations, and rationale from the systematic reviews.
Eighteen out of 27 questions received a consensus vote of ≥90%,
while the remaining nine recommendations failed to achieve this high
agreement threshold consensus vote, which was arbitrarily set by the
authors to manage the space limitations of JOR Consensus Articles.
Commentary on why consensus was not achieved and potential ways
forward are provided to stimulate specific funding mechanisms and
research on these critical MSKI host defense questions.
2 | METHODOLOGY
The main objective of the “Host Immunity” Section of the 2023 ORS
MSKI ICM was to bring together microbiologists, immunologists,
clinicians, and FDA regulators from around the world to assimilate
the best available data on preclinical research of host – pathogen
interactions during orthopedic infections. The first step, led by the
Section Chair, Dr. Edward M. Schwarz, was to identify and recruit
host immunity experts from the ORS RIG and beyond. These experts
agreed to serve as delegates tasked to identify the controversies and
challenges related to topical preclinical research, then seek consensus
on those issues using the Delphi methodology.
9 This has proven to be
a critical tool by which thought leaders debate all existing knowledge
to derive “general agreement ” in response to clinical care ‐driven
questions.
